walkthrough.title: Unfold the Map
walkthrough.text: Let's get Root set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. The map shows a woodland with twelve clearings connected by paths. Each clearing has a suit icon: fox, rabbit, or mouse. You will also notice some clearings marked with an R for ruins. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Assign Factions
walkthrough.text: Root is an asymmetric game, meaning every player plays a completely different faction with unique rules. For your first four-player game, we recommend: the Marquise de Cat, the Eyrie Dynasties, the Woodland Alliance, and the Vagabond. Randomly assign factions or let players choose. Give each player their faction board and all matching faction pieces.

walkthrough.title: Place Score Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their scoring marker and places it on the zero space of the victory point track that runs around the edge of the board. The first player to reach thirty victory points wins the game. Keep these markers visible to everyone throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Ruins
walkthrough.text: Find the four ruin tokens. Place one ruin token on each clearing marked with an R on the map. Ruins contain items that the Vagabond can discover by exploring. Leave the ruin tokens face down until the Vagabond interacts with them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Item Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the twelve item tokens on their matching spaces on the board's item supply track. There should be two boots, two swords, two bags, two crossbows, one hammer, one tea, two coins, and additional items. These items can be crafted by any faction using craft cards and are also found in ruins by the Vagabond.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Shared Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all the shared cards into one face-down deck. For two-player games, remove all four Dominance cards before shuffling. For three or four players, leave the Dominance cards in the deck. Place the deck and a discard area near the board where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal three cards from the shared deck to each player. Players may look at their own cards but should keep them hidden from opponents. Hand contents are private, but the number of cards in your hand is always public information.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Shared Components
walkthrough.text: Place the two custom dice near the board. Distribute the sixteen faction overview cards so players can reference any faction's abilities. These overview cards are helpful because every faction operates differently, and you will want to understand what your opponents can do.

walkthrough.title: Marquise Setup
walkthrough.text: The Marquise de Cat sets up first. Choose a corner clearing and place the Keep token there. Then place one warrior in every clearing on the map except the corner diagonally opposite your Keep. That means eleven clearings get a warrior each. Finally, place one sawmill, one workshop, and one recruiter among the Keep clearing and its adjacent clearings. Put remaining buildings on the faction board tracks.

walkthrough.title: Eyrie Setup
walkthrough.text: The Eyrie Dynasties set up next. Place six warriors and one roost in the corner clearing diagonally opposite the Marquise's Keep. This is your starting stronghold. Place the remaining roosts on your faction board track. Choose one of the four leader cards to lead the Eyrie this game and place it face up. Each leader has a different special ability.

walkthrough.title: Woodland Alliance Setup
walkthrough.text: The Woodland Alliance has a unique start. You do not place any pieces on the map yet. Instead, place all your warriors and sympathy tokens on your faction board. The Alliance builds support through sympathy and revolts, slowly gaining map presence. Take your three supporter cards and place them in the supporter stack area of your board.

walkthrough.title: Vagabond Setup
walkthrough.text: The Vagabond is a lone wanderer. Place your pawn in any forest on the map, not in a clearing. Take your starting items as listed on your character card. The Vagabond does not control clearings but moves through them, trading, questing, aiding other factions, and occasionally fighting.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine who goes first. Play proceeds clockwise from the starting player. Each player's turn follows three phases: Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening. The Marquise typically goes first in the setup order, followed by Eyrie, Alliance, then Vagabond.

walkthrough.title: Review Your Faction Board
walkthrough.text: Before the game begins, take a moment to read your faction board carefully. Each faction has unique rules for Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening phases. Your faction board tells you exactly what actions you can take and in what order. The faction boards are your primary reference for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Golden Rules Reminder
walkthrough.text: Three golden rules to remember. First, if a card's text conflicts with the rules reference, the card wins. Second, any rule that says you cannot do something is absolute and cannot be overridden. Third, agreements between players are non-binding, so do not rely on promises. With those in mind, you are ready to play. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Root is an asymmetric war game played over multiple rounds. Each player controls a unique faction with different goals, actions, and scoring methods. The first player to reach thirty victory points wins immediately. Alternatively, a player can win by fulfilling a Dominance card condition. Every turn follows three phases: Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Birdsong
walkthrough.text: Birdsong is the start of your turn. Each faction has specific Birdsong effects listed on their faction board. These might include adding cards to the decree, gaining supporters, or refreshing items. Resolve your Birdsong effects in the order listed on your board. Some Dominance win conditions are also checked at the start of Birdsong.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Daylight
walkthrough.text: Daylight is the action phase. During Daylight, you perform your faction's unique actions as well as the shared actions available to everyone: crafting, moving, and battling. Each faction's Daylight phase works differently. The Marquise gets three actions to spend freely. The Eyrie must follow their decree. The Alliance spreads sympathy. The Vagabond explores and aids.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure: Evening
walkthrough.text: Evening is the cleanup phase. Resolve your faction's Evening effects, which typically involve drawing cards, scoring points, and discarding down to hand limit. Most factions draw one card plus additional cards based on their board state. The standard hand limit is five cards, and you must discard down at the end of Evening.

walkthrough.title: Ruling a Clearing
walkthrough.text: Ruling a clearing is one of the most important concepts in Root. You rule a clearing if you have the most combined warriors and buildings there. Tokens and pawns do not count. If there is a tie, nobody rules the clearing. Ruling matters because you must rule the origin or destination clearing to move, and many faction abilities require ruling specific clearings.

walkthrough.title: Moving
walkthrough.text: To move, take any number of your warriors or your pawn and move them from one clearing to an adjacent clearing connected by a path. You must rule either the origin clearing, the destination clearing, or both. You cannot move if you do not rule either end of the path. The same piece can move multiple times in a turn if your faction allows it.

walkthrough.title: Battle Step One: Declaration
walkthrough.text: To battle, choose a clearing where you have warriors or a pawn, then choose a defender who also has pieces there. Only the attacker initiates the battle. Both players need pieces in the clearing for a battle to occur.

walkthrough.title: Battle Step Two: Ambush
walkthrough.text: Before dice are rolled, the defender may play an ambush card matching the clearing's suit. An ambush deals two immediate hits to the attacker before any dice are rolled. However, the attacker may then play their own matching ambush card to cancel the defender's ambush entirely. Bird cards are wild and can serve as any suit for ambush.

walkthrough.title: Battle Step Three: Roll Dice
walkthrough.text: Roll the two custom dice. The attacker takes the higher die result as their hits, and the defender takes the lower die result. Each hit removes one enemy piece. However, your hits are capped by the number of warriors you have in the clearing. If you have two warriors, you can deal at most two hits even if you rolled a three.

walkthrough.title: Battle Step Four: Extra Hits and Casualties
walkthrough.text: Some abilities grant extra hits that are not capped by warrior count. After totaling all hits, both sides remove pieces simultaneously. The owner of removed pieces chooses which pieces to remove, but warriors must be removed before buildings and tokens. Removing an enemy building or token earns one VP for the attacker.

walkthrough.title: Crafting
walkthrough.text: To craft a card, activate crafting pieces matching the suit icons shown on the card's cost. Your crafting pieces are determined by your faction. The Marquise uses workshops, the Eyrie uses roosts, and other factions have their own crafting sources. Each crafting piece's suit is determined by the suit of the clearing it occupies. A card can be crafted as an immediate effect or as a persistent effect that stays in play.

walkthrough.title: Card Suits and Bird Cards
walkthrough.text: Each card has a suit: fox, rabbit, mouse, or bird. Many game effects require spending cards of specific suits. Bird cards are wild and can count as any suit when spending, taking, or giving cards. However, if an effect specifically requires a bird card, non-bird cards cannot substitute. When forced to discard fox, rabbit, or mouse cards, your bird cards must be treated as the required suit.

walkthrough.title: The Marquise de Cat
walkthrough.text: The Marquise is the industrial powerhouse. They start controlling most of the map and score VP by building sawmills, workshops, and recruiters. Each building has a track on their faction board. As buildings are placed on the map, they reveal higher VP values on the track. The Marquise gets three actions per Daylight to battle, move, recruit warriors, build, or overwork sawmills.

walkthrough.title: The Eyrie Dynasties
walkthrough.text: The Eyrie is a bird empire governed by a decree. Each turn during Birdsong, you add one or two cards to your decree columns: Recruit, Move, Battle, and Build. During Daylight, you must execute every card in each column in order. If you ever cannot fully execute a column, you fall into turmoil: lose VP, discard your decree, and start fresh with a new leader. The Eyrie scores VP by building roosts.

walkthrough.title: The Woodland Alliance
walkthrough.text: The Alliance is a guerrilla insurgency. They spread sympathy tokens across clearings to score VP. When an opponent moves warriors into a clearing with your sympathy, they must give you a card. You can revolt in clearings with sympathy, placing a base and warriors while removing all enemy pieces. Bases let you recruit and train officers. The Alliance grows slowly but can explode into power through revolts.

walkthrough.title: The Vagabond
walkthrough.text: The Vagabond is a lone adventurer who does not control clearings. Instead, the Vagabond moves through forests and clearings using boots, fights using swords, and aids other factions by giving them cards. The Vagabond scores VP by questing, completing tasks that match a clearing's suit and an item. The Vagabond can also go hostile toward a faction, scoring VP for removing their warriors.

walkthrough.title: Scoring VP: Shared Methods
walkthrough.text: All factions score VP from two shared sources. First, removing an enemy building or token in battle earns one VP. Second, crafting item cards scores the VP printed on the card. Beyond these shared methods, each faction has unique scoring on their faction board. Understanding how your opponents score is critical for knowing when to interfere.

walkthrough.title: The Dominance System
walkthrough.text: Dominance cards offer an alternative win condition. During Daylight, if you have ten or more VP, you may play a Dominance card from your hand. Your score marker is removed from the track and you now win only by meeting the Dominance condition at the start of your Birdsong. Fox, rabbit, and mouse Dominance cards require ruling three clearings of that suit. Bird Dominance requires ruling two opposite corners.

walkthrough.title: Piece Limits and Placement
walkthrough.text: Each faction has a limited number of warriors, buildings, and tokens as shown on their faction board. If you run out of a piece type, you cannot place more. When an effect would place or remove more pieces than available, do as much as possible. Understanding your piece limits helps you plan your expansion and avoid overextending.

walkthrough.title: Table Politics
walkthrough.text: Root is a deeply political game. Agreements are non-binding by the rules, but negotiation is essential. If one player is clearly in the lead, the other players need to coordinate pressure. If the Marquise is running away with building VP, someone needs to remove their buildings. If the Alliance is about to revolt everywhere, someone needs to clear their sympathy. Pay attention to the scoreboard and talk to your opponents.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when a player reaches thirty VP or fulfills their Dominance condition at the start of Birdsong. If multiple players would reach thirty VP simultaneously, the current turn player wins. There is no final round. The game can end on anyone's turn, so always be aware of how close each player is to winning.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Woodland
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Root! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with all four base factions. The Marquise de Cat controls the map, the Eyrie Dynasties want to build roosts, the Woodland Alliance spreads sympathy, and the Vagabond explores the forest. Let's see how they interact.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Map
walkthrough.text: After setup, the Marquise has warriors spread across almost every clearing plus a Keep and three buildings. The Eyrie has six warriors and a roost in one corner. The Alliance has no map presence yet. The Vagabond is lurking in a forest. Each player has three cards in hand. The tension is already building.

walkthrough.title: Marquise's Turn: Birdsong
walkthrough.text: The Marquise goes first. During Birdsong, the Marquise places one wood token at each sawmill they control. Right now they have one sawmill, so one wood token is placed. Wood is the fuel for building more buildings.

walkthrough.title: Marquise's Turn: Daylight Actions
walkthrough.text: The Marquise has three actions to spend during Daylight. For their first action, they recruit by placing a warrior in a clearing with a recruiter. For their second action, they move warriors from one clearing to a neighboring one, preparing for expansion. For their third action, they build a new sawmill in a clearing they rule that has wood available.

walkthrough.title: Why Building Matters for the Marquise
walkthrough.text: Every building the Marquise places on the map reveals VP on their faction board track. As the tracks clear, each new building scores more points. This is the Marquise's main scoring engine. But buildings are also targets. If opponents destroy your buildings, you lose that map presence and they gain VP.

walkthrough.title: Eyrie's Turn: Birdsong
walkthrough.text: The Eyrie must add one or two cards from their hand to their decree during Birdsong. The decree has four columns: Recruit, Move, Battle, and Build. The Eyrie player adds a bird card to the Recruit column and a fox card to the Battle column. These cards are commitments that must be fulfilled during Daylight.

walkthrough.title: Eyrie's Turn: Executing the Decree
walkthrough.text: During Daylight, the Eyrie must execute every card in each decree column. The Recruit column has one bird card, so the Eyrie recruits one warrior to any clearing with a roost. The Move column is empty. The Battle column has one fox card, so the Eyrie must battle in a fox clearing. If they cannot find a legal target, disaster strikes.

walkthrough.title: Eyrie's Mistake: Decree Overcommitment
walkthrough.text: The Eyrie player added a fox card to Battle, but there are no enemy pieces in any fox clearing they occupy. They cannot execute the Battle column. This triggers turmoil. The Eyrie loses VP equal to the number of bird cards in the decree, their entire decree is discarded, and they must choose a new leader. One bad card placement cost them their entire turn's momentum.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Decree Carefully
walkthrough.text: The Eyrie's decree is powerful when it works but devastating when it fails. Before adding cards, make absolutely sure you can execute every column next turn. Bird cards are wild and go in any column, making them safer choices. Suit-specific cards lock you into needing a legal target of that suit. Start conservative with bird cards until you understand the decree rhythm.

walkthrough.title: Alliance's Turn: Spreading Sympathy
walkthrough.text: The Woodland Alliance does not start with map presence, so their first turns focus on spreading sympathy. During Daylight, the Alliance spends supporter cards to place sympathy tokens in clearings. Supporters are cards in the Alliance's special supporter stack, not their hand. Each sympathy token placed scores VP based on how many are already on the map.

walkthrough.title: Why Sympathy Is Dangerous
walkthrough.text: Sympathy tokens have a powerful deterrent effect. Whenever any other player moves warriors into a clearing containing Alliance sympathy, that player must give the Alliance one card matching the clearing's suit. This means moving into sympathized clearings costs opponents cards. It is like a tax on movement.

walkthrough.title: Vagabond's Turn: Moving and Exploring
walkthrough.text: The Vagabond starts in a forest and moves to an adjacent clearing by exhausting a boot item. Once in a clearing, the Vagabond explores a ruin by exhausting a torch item, gaining an item token. Items are the Vagabond's lifeblood: boots for movement, swords for battle, bags for carrying, and more.

walkthrough.title: Vagabond's Mistake: Aiding Without Purpose
walkthrough.text: The Vagabond decides to aid the Marquise by giving them a card. Aiding scores VP and improves your relationship with that faction. But the Vagabond aided the Marquise, who is already the most powerful faction on the board. This makes the Marquise even stronger while the Vagabond gains very little. The Vagabond should have aided a weaker faction or focused on questing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Aid Strategically
walkthrough.text: As the Vagabond, your aid decisions shape the game's politics. Aiding the leader makes them harder to stop. Aiding a struggling faction can create an ally who protects you. And if a faction becomes hostile to you, you score VP for removing their warriors but lose the ability to aid them. Choose your allies carefully based on the board state.

walkthrough.title: The Marquise's Mistake: Ignoring Sympathy
walkthrough.text: On the Marquise's second turn, they move warriors through a clearing with Alliance sympathy and must give the Alliance a card. They did not plan around the sympathy, giving the Alliance free resources. Then the Alliance revolts in that very clearing, destroying the Marquise's building and gaining a base. The Marquise lost a building, the Alliance gained map presence, and it all started because the Marquise ignored the sympathy tax.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Respect All Factions' Threats
walkthrough.text: In Root, every faction is dangerous in a different way. The Marquise's buildings score VP. The Eyrie's decree accelerates each turn. The Alliance's sympathy deters movement and sets up revolts. The Vagabond scores through quests and can go hostile. Ignoring any faction's threats gives them free reign to execute their strategy. Always be aware of what each opponent needs to win.

walkthrough.title: Eyrie's Mistake: Forgetting Movement Rules
walkthrough.text: After recovering from turmoil, the Eyrie tries to move warriors from a clearing where they have one warrior and one roost. But the Marquise has three warriors there, so the Eyrie does not rule the clearing. Without ruling the origin or destination, the Eyrie cannot move. The warriors are stuck.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Rule Before Moving
walkthrough.text: You must rule either the origin or the destination to move. Before planning any movement, count warriors and buildings in both clearings. If neither end is ruled by you, those warriors are trapped until you can shift the balance through recruitment or battle. This is one of the most commonly forgotten rules in Root.

walkthrough.title: How the Game Develops
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, the map becomes more contested. The Marquise builds their industrial machine while defending their territory. The Eyrie's decree grows more powerful but more fragile. The Alliance revolts and establishes bases. The Vagabond accumulates items and navigates the politics. The VP race tightens, and table talk becomes critical.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player taking their Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening phases. Watch the VP track constantly. When someone approaches twenty VP, the table needs to decide who polices them. Battle is how you remove buildings and tokens for VP and how you slow down the leader. Crafting item cards provides steady VP for everyone.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on your first practice round of Root! You have seen all four factions in action and learned from three common mistakes. The Eyrie overcommitted their decree and hit turmoil. The Vagabond aided the wrong faction. The Marquise ignored Alliance sympathy and lost a building to a revolt. Root is complex, but these lessons will serve you well.

walkthrough.title: Before You March
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for faction-specific strategy basics.

Second, reference the Appendix for key terms and faction summaries.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun in the woodland!

Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest beginner traps in Root. First, playing symmetrically, treating all factions the same way. Each faction has a completely different engine, and generic strategies will fail. Second, forgetting that you must rule a clearing to move out of it or into it. Third, spending bird cards recklessly. Birds are wild and incredibly flexible, but if you run out, you will miss critical suit requirements later. Fourth, ignoring the leader on the VP track and letting them cruise to thirty.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Faction Board
walkthrough.text: This cannot be stressed enough. Your faction board is your entire playbook. It tells you what happens during Birdsong, what actions you have in Daylight, and what happens in Evening. New players often miss effects or take illegal actions because they did not read their board carefully. Before your first turn, read every line on your faction board. Reference it every turn until the flow becomes natural.

walkthrough.title: Target Buildings and Tokens, Not Just Warriors
walkthrough.text: Removing an enemy building or token earns you one VP. Removing a warrior earns you nothing unless you are the Vagabond going hostile. This means smart battling focuses on clearings where the enemy has buildings or tokens you can destroy, not just clearings with lots of warriors. The Marquise's buildings, the Eyrie's roosts, and the Alliance's sympathy tokens are all high-value targets.

walkthrough.title: Watch the VP Track
walkthrough.text: Root is a race to thirty VP, and games can end suddenly. If a player hits twenty VP and nobody slows them down, they can often sprint to thirty in two or three turns. Check the VP track at the start of every turn. If someone is ahead, discuss with the table who is best positioned to slow them down. Policing the leader is a shared responsibility, and the player who ignores it often loses.

walkthrough.title: Suit Management
walkthrough.text: Cards are one of your most valuable resources. Every suit has specific uses: fox, rabbit, and mouse cards match clearings of those suits for crafting, spending, and ambush. Bird cards are wild. When deciding which cards to spend, think about what you might need in the next two turns. Do not spend your only fox card if you need to craft something in a fox clearing next turn.

walkthrough.title: Crafting for VP
walkthrough.text: Crafting item cards is a reliable way to score VP that every faction can access. The VP values printed on craftable items add up over the game. If you have the right crafting pieces in the right clearings, look for cards to craft. This is especially important for factions like the Marquise whose workshops serve as crafting pieces. Do not forget this secondary scoring path.

walkthrough.title: Negotiation Is a Tool
walkthrough.text: Even though agreements are non-binding, negotiation shapes the game. Convince opponents to attack someone else. Trade information about what cards you have. Promise to leave someone's territory alone in exchange for them targeting the leader. The most politically savvy player often has a significant advantage because they can redirect aggression away from themselves.

walkthrough.title: Learn All Four Factions
walkthrough.text: To play Root well, you need to understand not just your faction but all the others. Knowing how the Eyrie decree works helps you predict when they might hit turmoil. Understanding Alliance revolt conditions tells you when sympathy is about to become dangerous. Studying the Vagabond's quest scoring helps you know when they are about to surge in VP. Play each faction at least once to build this knowledge.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't play symmetrically — each faction's engine is unique
Don't forget ruling requirement for movement
Don't spend bird cards recklessly — they're wild and precious
Don't ignore the leader — letting someone cruise to 30 VP loses the game

summary.title: Read Your Faction Board

Read every line before turn 1
Reference it every turn until flow is natural

Removing warrior = 0 VP (except Vagabond hostile)
Focus battles on clearings with enemy cardboard

summary.title: Watch the VP Track
bullets: At 20+ VP, a player can sprint to 30 in 2-3 turns
Check VP track every turn
Policing the leader is a shared responsibility

summary.title: Suit Management
bullets: Fox/rabbit/mouse cards match clearing suits
Bird cards = wild, extremely flexible
Think 2 turns ahead before spending cards

summary.title: Crafting for VP
bullets: Item crafting = reliable VP for any faction
Match crafting pieces (in correct suit clearings) to card costs
Don't forget this secondary scoring path

summary.title: Negotiation Is a Tool
bullets: Agreements non-binding but still shape the game
Redirect aggression, trade information, form temporary alliances
Political savvy = significant advantage

summary.title: Learn All Four Factions
bullets: Understanding opponents' engines = knowing when to interfere
Predict Eyrie turmoil, Alliance revolts, Vagabond VP surges
Play each faction at least once

walkthrough.title: Marquise: Building Tempo
walkthrough.text: The Marquise's strength is their early map control, but it erodes fast. Your priority is building tempo: get sawmills producing wood, build workshops for crafting VP, and place recruiters to replace lost warriors. The building track VP accelerates as you clear more slots. Try to place two buildings per turn by managing wood efficiently. Use your Keep's protection to safeguard key buildings.

walkthrough.title: Eyrie: Decree Management
walkthrough.text: The Eyrie decree is your greatest weapon and biggest liability. The safest approach is to add bird cards early, since they can execute in any clearing. Only add suit-specific cards when you are confident the target will exist. Keep your decree lean in the first few turns. As the decree grows, your power accelerates, but one misfire sends you into turmoil. Monitor the board state before every Birdsong to ensure executability.

walkthrough.title: Alliance: Revolt Timing
walkthrough.text: The Alliance is slow to start but explosive when they revolt. Place sympathy in clearings where opponents have valuable buildings, creating pressure. Save supporter cards for the revolt itself, which requires spending cards matching the clearing's suit. A successful revolt destroys all enemy pieces in the clearing and establishes a base, which unlocks recruitment and officer training. Time your revolts for maximum destruction.

walkthrough.title: Vagabond: Quest Efficiency
walkthrough.text: The Vagabond scores VP primarily through quests and exploration. Each quest requires being in a clearing of the right suit and exhausting the right item. Plan your movement to chain multiple quests efficiently. Items are your lifeblood, so explore ruins early for free items. The tea item refreshes exhausted items, effectively giving you extra actions. Prioritize boots for movement and swords for self-defense.

walkthrough.title: Policing the Leader
walkthrough.text: In a multiplayer game, the player in last place should not be the only one attacking the leader. Policing is a collective responsibility. If the Marquise is at twenty-two VP and building fast, everyone needs to contribute to slowing them down. The player best positioned to remove buildings should do so. The player with ambush cards should hold them for defense. Communicate at the table about who handles what.

walkthrough.title: Dominance Card Pivots
walkthrough.text: Dominance cards are an advanced win condition. Activating one at ten VP removes you from the VP race entirely, which means opponents cannot stop you by destroying your buildings for VP. You now win by ruling specific clearings at the start of your Birdsong. This can be powerful when you control a region but your VP scoring has stalled. However, opponents will ruthlessly target your required clearings, so only pivot to Dominance if you can defend.

walkthrough.title: Battle Math
walkthrough.text: Understanding battle math is critical. The attacker gets the higher of two dice, averaging about two point four hits. The defender gets the lower, averaging about one point six. Your hits are capped by your warrior count. This means attacking with just one warrior caps your damage at one even if you roll a three. Bring at least three warriors to a battle if you want full dice value. Ambush cards from the defender deal two pre-dice hits, making small attacks very risky.

walkthrough.title: Card Economy Mastery
walkthrough.text: Cards are the hidden currency of Root. Every card can be spent for crafting, used for ambush, added to the Eyrie decree, spent as Alliance supporters, or given for Vagabond aid. The opportunity cost of each card use is enormous. Before spending any card, ask yourself: is this the highest-value use of this card right now? Could I use it better next turn? Card advantage often determines the winner in close games.

walkthrough.title: Map Control Zones
walkthrough.text: Think of the map in zones rather than individual clearings. Control a cluster of connected clearings to create a safe zone where you can move freely, build without threat, and craft using your pieces. Deny opponents contiguous zones by placing warriors or sympathy in key chokepoint clearings. The player who controls the best zone usually has the most freedom to execute their strategy.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: Root endgames are sudden. When any player reaches twenty-five VP, every other player should be in crisis mode. Calculate the leader's maximum VP gain per turn. Can they craft two items? Remove two buildings? Build three structures? If their maximum exceeds the gap to thirty, you must act this turn. The most common way to lose Root is realizing one turn too late that the leader was about to win.

entries.definition: You rule a clearing if you have the most combined warriors and buildings there. Tokens and pawns do not count. Ties mean no one rules. Required for movement and many abilities.
One of the twelve map locations where factions place warriors, buildings, and tokens. Each clearing has a suit (fox, rabbit, or mouse) and connects to adjacent clearings via paths.
The spaces between clearings on the map. Only the Vagabond uses forests for movement and hiding. Other factions cannot move through or place pieces in forests.
The lines connecting adjacent clearings. Movement must follow paths. You can only move between clearings connected by a path.
The basic piece for most factions. Warriors count toward ruling a clearing and cap your battle hits. Each faction has a limited supply of warriors on their faction board.
Faction-specific structures placed in clearings. Count toward ruling. Each clearing has limited building slots. Removing an enemy building earns the attacker one VP.
Faction-specific markers placed on the map (like sympathy or wood). Tokens do not count toward ruling. Removing an enemy token earns the attacker one VP.
The Vagabond's single piece on the map. Does not count toward ruling. Moves through clearings and forests using boot items.
The first phase of each turn. Resolve faction-specific effects in listed order. Dominance win conditions are checked at the start of Birdsong.
The second phase and main action phase. Perform faction-unique actions plus shared actions (Craft, Move, Battle).
The third and final phase. Draw cards, score faction VP, discard down to hand limit of five. Draw count varies by faction board state.
A shared action to play a card for its effect by activating crafting pieces matching the card's cost suits. Each crafting piece's suit equals its clearing's suit.
A shared action to move warriors or your pawn between adjacent clearings. Must rule origin or destination. Multiple pieces can move together.
A shared action to fight in a clearing. Attacker takes higher die, defender takes lower. Hits capped by warrior count. Removing buildings/tokens earns VP.
A card play during battle. Defender plays a matching-suit card to deal two hits before dice. Attacker can play a matching card to cancel. Bird cards are wild.
The industrial faction. Starts controlling most of the map. Scores VP by building sawmills, workshops, and recruiters. Gets three Daylight actions. Wood tokens fuel building.
The bird empire governed by a decree. Adds cards to decree each Birdsong, must execute all in Daylight. Failure triggers turmoil. Scores VP by building roosts.
The guerrilla insurgency. Spreads sympathy tokens for VP and to tax opponent movement. Revolts destroy all enemy pieces and establish bases. Grows slowly but explosively.
The lone adventurer. Does not control clearings. Uses items for actions. Scores VP through quests, exploration, and aiding. Can go hostile toward factions.
The Eyrie's unique mechanic. Cards are added each Birdsong to four columns (Recruit, Move, Battle, Build). Every card must be executed in Daylight or turmoil occurs.
When the Eyrie cannot execute their full decree. Lose VP equal to bird cards in decree, discard entire decree, choose a new leader. A devastating setback.
Woodland Alliance tokens placed in clearings. Score VP when placed. Tax opponents who move warriors into sympathized clearings by forcing them to give the Alliance a card.
Alliance action that destroys all enemy pieces in a clearing with sympathy and places a base and warriors. Requires spending matching-suit supporter cards.
Alliance buildings established through revolt. Unlock recruitment and officer training. If destroyed, the Alliance loses all supporters of the base's suit.
The Marquise's unique starting token placed in a corner clearing. Prevents other factions from placing pieces in that clearing.
Eyrie buildings that enable recruitment and provide crafting. Building roosts reveals VP on the Eyrie's faction board track.
The Vagabond's primary VP source. Complete a quest by being in a matching-suit clearing and exhausting two required items. Gain VP and choose a reward.
The Vagabond gives a card to another faction to improve their relationship and score VP. Aiding strengthens the relationship track between the Vagabond and that faction.
When the Vagabond's relationship with a faction becomes hostile (usually by removing their warriors), the Vagabond scores VP for each warrior of that faction removed but can no longer aid them.
An alternative win condition card. Playable during Daylight at ten or more VP. Removes you from the VP track. Win by meeting the card's map control condition at the start of your Birdsong.
A card with the bird suit that counts as any other suit when spending, taking, or giving cards. Cannot substitute when a bird card is specifically required. Extremely flexible.
Every card and clearing has a suit: fox, rabbit, mouse, or bird. Suits determine crafting costs, ambush eligibility, supporter requirements, and clearing interactions.
Four tokens placed in R-marked clearings during setup. The Vagabond explores ruins to gain items. Each ruin contains one item token.
Twelve items on the board's supply track. Items can be crafted from cards by any faction or found in ruins by the Vagabond. Types include boots, swords, bags, crossbows, hammer, tea, and coins.
The primary scoring currency. First to thirty wins. Earned by removing enemy buildings/tokens, crafting items, and faction-specific scoring. Tracked on the board's VP track.
Each player's unique reference board listing their Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening phases, piece tracks, scoring systems, and special rules. The most important reference in the game.
The social dynamic of Root. Agreements are non-binding. Negotiation shapes who attacks whom and when. Policing the leader is a shared responsibility. Communication is a tool.
